source_text: Subject: Supply security & production capacity for critical medicines and ingredients
article_text:
recital 3
Shortages of medicinal products can have very different and complex root causes, with challenges identified along the entire pharmaceutical value chain. In particular, shortages of medicinal products can result from supply chain disruptions and vulnerabilities affecting the supply of key ingredients and components. These include existing dependencies on a limited number of suppliers globally and lack of Union capacities to produce certain medicinal products, their active substances or key raw pharmaceutical materials. Through diversification of supply sources and investment in local production, the Union can reduce its risk of exposure to shortages of medicinal products.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Medicines for Europe's pre-proposal text focused on supply security and production capacity for critical medicines and ingredients, which directly aligns with Recital 3's discussion of supply chain disruptions, dependencies on limited suppliers, and lack of Union production capacities as root causes of medicine shortages.

source_text: Subject: critical medicines act
article_text:
article 25
Establishment of Critical Medicines Coordination Group 1.A Critical Medicines Coordination Group (‘Critical Medicines Group’) is hereby established. 2.The Member States and the Commission are Members of the Critical Medicines Group. Each Member State shall appoint a maximum of two high-level permanent representatives, with the expertise relevant for implementing all the different measures set out in this Regulation. Where relevant as regards the function and expertise, Member States may appoint different representatives in relation to different tasks of the Critical Medicines Group. Appointed permanent representatives shall ensure the necessary coordination within their respective Member State. The Agency shall have an observer status. 3.The Critical Medicines Group shall work closely with the MSSG, the Agency, and national authorities responsible for medicinal products. For discussions where input from the medicines regulatory authorities’ perspective is necessary, the Critical Medicines Group may organise joint meetings with the MSSG. 4.The Commission shall organise and coordinate the work of the Critical Medicines Group by means of the Secretariat. 5.A representative of the Commission shall chair the meetings of the Critical Medicines Group. 6.The Critical Medicines Group, at the proposal of the Chair or any its members, may decide to establish a working group. 7.The Critical Medicines Group shall use its best endeavours to reach consensus, where possible. Members with diverging positions may request that their positions and the grounds on which they are based be recorded in the Critical Medicines Group’s position.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The pre-proposal text references the 'critical medicines act' only as a subject label, with no specific positional stance on the establishment or structure of a Critical Medicines Coordination Group as described in Article 25. The overlap is purely topical.

source_text: Subject: critical medicines act
article_text:
article 25
Establishment of Critical Medicines Coordination Group 1.A Critical Medicines Coordination Group (‘Critical Medicines Group’) is hereby established. 2.The Member States and the Commission are Members of the Critical Medicines Group. Each Member State shall appoint a maximum of two high-level permanent representatives, with the expertise relevant for implementing all the different measures set out in this Regulation. Where relevant as regards the function and expertise, Member States may appoint different representatives in relation to different tasks of the Critical Medicines Group. Appointed permanent representatives shall ensure the necessary coordination within their respective Member State. The Agency shall have an observer status. 3.The Critical Medicines Group shall work closely with the MSSG, the Agency, and national authorities responsible for medicinal products. For discussions where input from the medicines regulatory authorities’ perspective is necessary, the Critical Medicines Group may organise joint meetings with the MSSG. 4.The Commission shall organise and coordinate the work of the Critical Medicines Group by means of the Secretariat. 5.A representative of the Commission shall chair the meetings of the Critical Medicines Group. 6.The Critical Medicines Group, at the proposal of the Chair or any its members, may decide to establish a working group. 7.The Critical Medicines Group shall use its best endeavours to reach consensus, where possible. Members with diverging positions may request that their positions and the grounds on which they are based be recorded in the Critical Medicines Group’s position.
label: NOISE
reasoning: As with match 2, the pre-proposal text from von der Leyen's cabinet references the 'critical medicines act' as a subject label only, without expressing any substantive position on the Critical Medicines Coordination Group established in Article 25. The overlap is coincidental and topical.

source_text: It is composed of three phases: supply chain transparency, stress tests, and the development of short, medium and long term resilience-enhancing measures. At the same time, the European Commission, in its own studies and reports, has strong evidence that underlines the strategic dependencies of Europe on APIs and essential medicines and that the structural root cause of shortages lies in the pricing and procurement of these medicines: • European Commission Staff Working Document on strategic dependencies and capacities (May 2021). • The Technopolis study ‘’Future-proofing pharmaceutical legislation Study on medicine shortages : final report’’ (December 2021). • Study on best practices in public procurement of medicines (Gesundheit Österreich GmbH) (September 2022). Rue d’Arlon 50 - 1000 Brussels - Belgium T: +32 (0)2 736 84 11- F: +32 (0)2 736 74 38 www.medicinesforeurope.com 1
article_text:
recital 30
The procurement of medicinal products is organised differently across Member States, involving various actors. To strengthen the security of supply chains for critical medicinal products, Member States should establish national programmes that promote the consistent use of procurement criteria by contracting authorities within their territory, including the application of multi-winner approaches where beneficial, based on thorough market analysis. To ensure a comprehensive approach, and considering that critical medicinal products are also relevant for outpatient sector where they are often not purchased through public procurement, these programmes may also encompass measures to strengthen supply chain resilience and sustainability through measures related to pricing and reimbursement, where appropriate. The programmes should be shared with the Commission and the Critical Medicines Coordination Group, established by this Regulation, to facilitate the exchange of best practices and coordination between the Member States. This cooperation should enhance the overall effectiveness of the various measures put forward to secure the supply of critical medicinal products, while respecting the principles of subsidiarity and proportionality.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Medicines for Europe's feedback explicitly identifies pricing and procurement as structural root causes of shortages, and Recital 30 directly addresses procurement of medicinal products and the need for Member States to establish national procurement programmes — reflecting alignment on the centrality of procurement reform to supply security.

source_text: It is composed of three phases: supply chain transparency, stress tests, and the development of short, medium and long term resilience-enhancing measures. At the same time, the European Commission, in its own studies and reports, has strong evidence that underlines the strategic dependencies of Europe on APIs and essential medicines and that the structural root cause of shortages lies in the pricing and procurement of these medicines: • European Commission Staff Working Document on strategic dependencies and capacities (May 2021). • The Technopolis study ‘’Future-proofing pharmaceutical legislation Study on medicine shortages : final report’’ (December 2021). • Study on best practices in public procurement of medicines (Gesundheit Österreich GmbH) (September 2022). Rue d’Arlon 50 - 1000 Brussels - Belgium T: +32 (0)2 736 84 11- F: +32 (0)2 736 74 38 www.medicinesforeurope.com 1
article_text:
recital 7
However, despite regulatory obligations on marketing authorisation holders to ensure the continuous supply of medicinal products to meet patients’ demand and the additional regulatory mechanism introduced by Regulation of the European Parliament and of the Council (EU) 2022/123 and Regulation (EU) …/… [reference to be added after adoption cf. COM(2023) 193 final] to mitigate and respond to shortages, the functioning of markets alone does not always guarantee the availability of medicinal products. This risk is particularly evident in cases of supply chain disruptions, especially when the supply of a given medicinal product relies on a limited number of global suppliers and production facilities or where there is a high dependency on a single or a limited number of third countries.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation's text highlights strategic dependencies and structural market failures as root causes of shortages, which aligns with Recital 7's acknowledgment that market functioning alone does not guarantee medicine availability and that supply chain disruptions pose particular risks.

source_text: It is composed of three phases: supply chain transparency, stress tests, and the development of short, medium and long term resilience-enhancing measures. At the same time, the European Commission, in its own studies and reports, has strong evidence that underlines the strategic dependencies of Europe on APIs and essential medicines and that the structural root cause of shortages lies in the pricing and procurement of these medicines: • European Commission Staff Working Document on strategic dependencies and capacities (May 2021). • The Technopolis study ‘’Future-proofing pharmaceutical legislation Study on medicine shortages : final report’’ (December 2021). • Study on best practices in public procurement of medicines (Gesundheit Österreich GmbH) (September 2022). Rue d’Arlon 50 - 1000 Brussels - Belgium T: +32 (0)2 736 84 11- F: +32 (0)2 736 74 38 www.medicinesforeurope.com 1
article_text:
recital 16
To ensure that the measures are applied where justified and proportionate, it is necessary to demonstrate that some measures address a vulnerability in the supply chains of a given critical medicinal product. This Regulation should rely on the vulnerability evaluation performed for the purpose of the application of the general pharmaceutical legislation as per Regulation (EU) No …/… [reference to be added after adoption cf. COM(2023) 193 final]. To detect a vulnerability in the supply chains it is necessary to look at aggregated data across all medicinal products authorised in the Union and containing the same active substance, route of administration and formulation. Such an approach allows for the determination whether, for a critical medicinal product with a given active substance, the Union is highly dependent on a single or a limited number of third countries, or a limited number of sites, for active substances, key inputs, or finished dosage forms.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Medicines for Europe's emphasis on supply chain transparency and stress-testing for resilience aligns with Recital 16's focus on vulnerability evaluation of supply chains for critical medicinal products as a prerequisite for applying proportionate resilience measures.

source_text: Subject: EU health policy Upon their request, the Cabinet held an introductory meeting with the representatives of Novartis. The Cabinet provided an overview of the main priorities of the Commission in the field of health policy for the upcoming period . The representatives of Novartis outlined their key priorities as regards the pharmaceutical legislation, critical medicines, biotechnology, as well as cardiovascular health.
article_text:
recital 37
Ensuring a structured and coordinated approach to strengthening the security of supply of critical medicinal products requires collaboration between the Member States and the Commission. To facilitate this, the Critical Medicines Coordination Group (‘the Critical Medicines Group’) should be established to facilitate effective coordination across the relevant policy areas. The Critical Medicines Group should be composed of high-level representatives of Member States with expertise in medicinal product procurement policies, industrial policy related to pharmaceuticals and public health. The Commission should be a member of the group. To ensure structured discussions, the Commission should chair the Critical Medicines Group and perform the functions of its secretariat.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The source text only notes that Novartis raised critical medicines as a topic, without expressing any specific position on a coordination group structure; the overlap is therefore purely topical.

source_text: Subject: EU health policy Upon their request, the Cabinet held an introductory meeting with the representatives of Novartis. The Cabinet provided an overview of the main priorities of the Commission in the field of health policy for the upcoming period . The representatives of Novartis outlined their key priorities as regards the pharmaceutical legislation, critical medicines, biotechnology, as well as cardiovascular health.
article_text:
article 27
Strategic partnerships Without prejudice to the prerogatives of the Council, the Commission, shall explore possibilities of concluding strategic partnerships aiming to diversify sourcing of critical medicinal products, their active substances and key inputs to increase the security of supply of critical medicinal products in the Union. The Commission shall also explore the possibility of building on existing forms of cooperation, when possible, to support security of supply and reinforce efforts to strengthen the production of critical medicinal products in the Union. Chapter VII Amendments to Regulation (EU) 2024/795
label: NOISE
reasoning: Although Novartis listed critical medicines and pharmaceutical legislation as priorities, the source text records no specific stance on strategic partnerships or diversification of sourcing. The overlap with Article 27 is topical rather than reflecting any expressed position by Novartis.

source_text:
Subject: Upcoming Commission initiatives related to biotech and biomanufacturing - EuropaBio presented their activities.
- EuropaBio stressed the potential of biotech in addressing a broad range of strategic objectives of the EU, including competitiveness, economic resilience, sustainability and industrial transition. As a strong enabling technology, biotech should be part of the upcoming Commission initiatives, including the Biotech Act, the strategy for European Life Sciences and the Bioeconomy Strategy update.
- EuropaBio underlined the need to have a broad scope for the Biotech Act to bring benefits across industrial sectors.
- For EuropaBio, a s peedier access to markets is crucial. There is scope to accelerate the procedures within the existing legislative framework to get innovations to the market quicker without putting health and safety at risk.
- For EuropaBio, r egulatory convergence globally, e.g., aligning good manufacturing practices, would reduce costs and facilitate the movement of products between countries and regions and help attracting investments in the EU.
- EuropaBio highlighted the potential of i ndustrial biotech sector to valorise feedstocks and create sustainable bio - based value chains in regions . Regulatory predictability/certainty is needed to incentivise this long-term work (deployment of technologies, feedstock availability, market pull).
- For EuropaBio, the upcoming Critical Medicines Act (CMA) should be well -balanced, focused on key challenges and aim at accelerating the markets access of innovative bio -based therapies.
- COM took note of the comments , presented a short overview of the upcoming initiatives including the Strategy for European Life Sciences, update of the Bioeconomy Strategy and Biotech Act and encouraged EuropaBio to participate in the public consultations on these initiatives.
article_text:
recital 22
Union-level funding may be leveraged to facilitate investments in strategic projects. Strategic projects may benefit from access to existing EU funding instruments, such as the EU4Health Programme , Digital Europe Programme and Horizon Europe (relevant, for example, for active substances referred to in Article 5(d) of Regulation (EU)2021/695), as well as the Strategic Technologies for Europe Platform (STEP), when they fulfil the criteria established in these instruments. Authorities in charge of the Union programmes covered by Regulation (EU) 2024/795 of the European Parliament and of the Council (STEP) should in particular consider supporting strategic projects addressing a vulnerability in the supply chains of critical medicinal products and therefore Regulation (EU) 2024/795 should be amended.
label: NOISE
reasoning: EuropaBio's pre-proposal text advocates for a broad scope for the Biotech Act and biotech's role in EU strategic objectives, while Recital 22 deals with EU-level funding instruments for strategic projects in critical medicines. The overlap is purely topical (EU strategic investment) with no alignment on EuropaBio's specific position about biotech's broad enabling role.

source_text:
Subject: Upcoming Commission initiatives related to biotech and biomanufacturing - EuropaBio presented their activities.
- EuropaBio stressed the potential of biotech in addressing a broad range of strategic objectives of the EU, including competitiveness, economic resilience, sustainability and industrial transition. As a strong enabling technology, biotech should be part of the upcoming Commission initiatives, including the Biotech Act, the strategy for European Life Sciences and the Bioeconomy Strategy update.
- EuropaBio underlined the need to have a broad scope for the Biotech Act to bring benefits across industrial sectors.
- For EuropaBio, a s peedier access to markets is crucial. There is scope to accelerate the procedures within the existing legislative framework to get innovations to the market quicker without putting health and safety at risk.
- For EuropaBio, r egulatory convergence globally, e.g., aligning good manufacturing practices, would reduce costs and facilitate the movement of products between countries and regions and help attracting investments in the EU.
- EuropaBio highlighted the potential of i ndustrial biotech sector to valorise feedstocks and create sustainable bio - based value chains in regions . Regulatory predictability/certainty is needed to incentivise this long-term work (deployment of technologies, feedstock availability, market pull).
- For EuropaBio, the upcoming Critical Medicines Act (CMA) should be well -balanced, focused on key challenges and aim at accelerating the markets access of innovative bio -based therapies.
- COM took note of the comments , presented a short overview of the upcoming initiatives including the Strategy for European Life Sciences, update of the Bioeconomy Strategy and Biotech Act and encouraged EuropaBio to participate in the public consultations on these initiatives.
article_text:
article 28
Regulation (EU) 2024/795 is amended as follows: (a)in Article 2, (1) point (a), subparagraph (iii) is replaced by the following: ‘(iii)biotechnologies, and any other technologies relevant for manufacturing of critical medicinal products as defined in Critical Medicines Act *; _________ *Regulation (EU) … of the European Parliament and of the Council laying down a framework for strengthening the availability and security of supply of critical medicinal products as well as for improving the availability of, and access to, medicinal products of common interest, and amending Regulation (EU) 2024/795.’ [D.G.: reference to be completed with the definitive title of the ‘Critical Medicines Act’ and with its publications references once they are available];’ (b)in Article 2, the following subparagraph is added in paragraph 3: ‘By way of derogation from the first subparagraph of this paragraph, the value chain for the development or manufacturing of medicinal products that fall within the scope of the [Critical Medicines Act] and that are referred to in paragraph 1, point (a)(iii) of this Article, relates to finished dosage forms, as well as to active pharmaceutical ingredients and other key inputs necessary for the production of the finished dosage forms of critical medicinal products as defined in the Regulation.’; (c)in article 2, paragraph 8 is added: ‘8.Strategic projects designated in accordance with the [Critical Medicines Act] that address a vulnerability in the supply chains of critical medicinal products shall be deemed to contribute to the STEP objective referred to in paragraph 1, point (a)(iii).’; (d)in Article 4, paragraph 7 is replaced by the following: ‘7.Strategic projects recognised in accordance with the relevant provisions of the Net-Zero Industry Act, the Critical Raw Materials Act [and the Critical Medicines Act] that fall within the scope of Article 2 of this Regulation and that receive a contribution under the programmes referred to in Article 3 of this Regulation may also receive a contribution from any other Union programme, including funds under shared management, provided that those contributions do not cover the same costs. The rules of the relevant Union programme shall apply to the corresponding contribution to the strategic project. The cumulative funding shall not exceed the total eligible costs of the strategic project. The support from the different Union programmes may be calculated on a pro rata basis in accordance with the documents setting out the conditions for support.’; (e)in Article 6, paragraph 1, point c is replaced by the following: (c)details of projects that have been recognized as strategic projects under the Net-Zero Industry Act, the Critical Raw Materials Act and the [Critical Medicines Act], to the extent that they fall within the scope of Article 2 of this Regulation. Chapter VIII Final provisions
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: EuropaBio explicitly called for biotechnologies to be included in upcoming Commission initiatives and the Biotech Act, and Article 28 directly amends Regulation (EU) 2024/795 to include 'biotechnologies' as a strategic technology relevant for manufacturing critical medicinal products, reflecting EuropaBio's position on integrating biotech into EU strategic frameworks.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2025)1547319 - 26/02/2025 EuropaBio response to the Call for Evidence on the Critical Medicines Act (26.02.2025) EuropaBio, the industry association for biotechnology in Europe, welcomes the opportunity to provide feedback on the future Critical Medicines Act (CMA). Strengthening the security of supply and availability of critical medicinal products in the EU is essential to ensure resilience and autonomy. EuropaBio believes that achieving these objectives broader policy agenda aimed at boosting the competitiveness of the wider biotech and pharmaceutical value chains. This agenda should reflect the need for resilient global supply chains for the biotech and pharmaceutical industries, including beyond manufacturers. General Comments In line with the Strategic Report of the Critical Medicines Alliance, it is essential that the CMA duly reflects the differences between the innovative and off-patent industries as regards resilience. In that respect, EuropaBio believes that the scope of the Act should be focused on where vulnerabilities exist and additional requirements on companies should be imposed on a risk-based approach only in cases where companies MAHs have experienced significant shortages and do not have implemented mitigation strategies. EuropaBio believes that medicines for rare diseases and for small populations should be specifically excluded from the scope of the CMA due to their unique characteristics and heterogeneity of the patient population
article_text:
recital 7
However, despite regulatory obligations on marketing authorisation holders to ensure the continuous supply of medicinal products to meet patients’ demand and the additional regulatory mechanism introduced by Regulation of the European Parliament and of the Council (EU) 2022/123 and Regulation (EU) …/… [reference to be added after adoption cf. COM(2023) 193 final] to mitigate and respond to shortages, the functioning of markets alone does not always guarantee the availability of medicinal products. This risk is particularly evident in cases of supply chain disruptions, especially when the supply of a given medicinal product relies on a limited number of global suppliers and production facilities or where there is a high dependency on a single or a limited number of third countries.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: EuropaBio's feedback stressed the importance of strengthening security of supply and availability of critical medicinal products for EU resilience, which is directly echoed in Recital 7's acknowledgment that market functioning alone does not guarantee medicinal product availability and that supply chain disruptions pose risks — consistent with EuropaBio's call for action.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2025)1547319 - 26/02/2025 EuropaBio response to the Call for Evidence on the Critical Medicines Act (26.02.2025) EuropaBio, the industry association for biotechnology in Europe, welcomes the opportunity to provide feedback on the future Critical Medicines Act (CMA). Strengthening the security of supply and availability of critical medicinal products in the EU is essential to ensure resilience and autonomy. EuropaBio believes that achieving these objectives broader policy agenda aimed at boosting the competitiveness of the wider biotech and pharmaceutical value chains. This agenda should reflect the need for resilient global supply chains for the biotech and pharmaceutical industries, including beyond manufacturers. General Comments In line with the Strategic Report of the Critical Medicines Alliance, it is essential that the CMA duly reflects the differences between the innovative and off-patent industries as regards resilience. In that respect, EuropaBio believes that the scope of the Act should be focused on where vulnerabilities exist and additional requirements on companies should be imposed on a risk-based approach only in cases where companies MAHs have experienced significant shortages and do not have implemented mitigation strategies. EuropaBio believes that medicines for rare diseases and for small populations should be specifically excluded from the scope of the CMA due to their unique characteristics and heterogeneity of the patient population
article_text:
recital 5
To enhance the security of supply for medicinal products and thereby contribute to a high level of public health protection, the Union has implemented a range of measures that contribute to building a European Health Union. In particular, Regulation (EU) 2022/123 of the European Parliament and of the Council has reinforced the European Medicines Agency’s (‘the Agency’) mandate by enhancing monitoring, coordination, and reporting mechanisms to prevent and mitigate supply disruptions of critical medicinal products across Member States. That Regulation also established the Agency’s Executive Steering Group on Shortages and Safety of Medicinal Products (‘the MSSG’), which brings together representatives from the Agency and Member States, to coordinate urgent actions within the Union to manage existing shortages and issues related to the quality, safety, and efficacy of medicinal products.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: EuropaBio welcomed the Critical Medicines Act and emphasized the need to strengthen security of supply and availability of critical medicinal products, which is directly reflected in Recital 5's description of EU measures to enhance security of supply and build a European Health Union through reinforced EMA mandates and monitoring mechanisms.

label: ALIGNED
reasoning: EuropaBio's feedback emphasized that measures should be proportionate and targeted within a broader competitiveness agenda, and Recital 16 aligns with this by stating that measures must be justified and proportionate, relying on vulnerability evaluations to detect supply chain weaknesses before applying interventions.

source_text: Subject: Role of full line wholesalers in the safe supply of medicines in the EU The meeting focused on the critical role of pharmaceutical wholesalers in ensuring medicine supply across the EU. GIRP underscored the critical role that wholesalers play in the EU pharmaceutical supply chain, highlighting their essential contributions to public healthcare. GIRP provided an overview of the association’s role and the wholesalers' impact on medicine supply in the EU. The Commissioner acknowledged the challenges and outlined the Commission’s perspective. He confirmed his commitment to implementing the Critical Medicines Act within the first 100 days to address market failures and create viable national markets. 2 The meeting reaffirmed the importance of wholesalers in ensuring medicine supply and healthcare resilience.
article_text:
recital 7
However, despite regulatory obligations on marketing authorisation holders to ensure the continuous supply of medicinal products to meet patients’ demand and the additional regulatory mechanism introduced by Regulation of the European Parliament and of the Council (EU) 2022/123 and Regulation (EU) …/… [reference to be added after adoption cf. COM(2023) 193 final] to mitigate and respond to shortages, the functioning of markets alone does not always guarantee the availability of medicinal products. This risk is particularly evident in cases of supply chain disruptions, especially when the supply of a given medicinal product relies on a limited number of global suppliers and production facilities or where there is a high dependency on a single or a limited number of third countries.
label: NOISE
reasoning: Both texts discuss the role of wholesalers and supply chain challenges in the EU pharmaceutical sector, but the article focuses on market failures and regulatory gaps without specifically acknowledging wholesalers' critical role as GIRP advocates. The overlap is topical rather than a reflection of GIRP's position.

The procurement of medicinal products is organised differently across Member States, involving various actors. To strengthen the security of supply chains for critical medicinal products, Member States should establish national programmes that promote the consistent use of procurement criteria by contracting authorities within their territory, including the application of multi-winner approaches where beneficial, based on thorough market analysis. To ensure a comprehensive approach, and considering that critical medicinal products are also relevant for outpatient sector where they are often not purchased through public procurement, these programmes may also encompass measures to strengthen supply chain resilience and sustainability through measures related to pricing and reimbursement, where appropriate. The programmes should be shared with the Commission and the Critical Medicines Coordination Group, established by this Regulation, to facilitate the exchange of best practices and coordination between the Member States. This cooperation should enhance the overall effectiveness of the various measures put forward to secure the supply of critical medicinal products, while respecting the principles of subsidiarity and proportionality.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: GIRP's pre-proposal text emphasises the critical role of pharmaceutical wholesalers in ensuring a resilient supply chain, and the article explicitly calls for Member States to establish national programmes using procurement criteria that strengthen supply chain security for critical medicines — aligning with GIRP's advocacy for wholesaler integration in procurement frameworks.

source_text: European Healthcare Distribution Association the vital link in healthcare Groupement International de la Répartition Pharmaceutique b. The reporting of stock levels for critical medicines under the stockpiling obligation can be mandated if required by public health authorities. Before reporting, MAHs and/or outsourced third parties must receive assurances that their IP protected data and trading information will not be compromised. Conclusion The Critical Medicines Act represents a crucial opportunity to strengthen the resilience of Europe’s pharmaceutical supply chain by addressing the root causes of supply disruptions—most notably, the complexity and vulnerabilities within upstream supply chains. Pharmaceutical full-line wholesalers play a pivotal role in ensuring that once medicines are made available, they reach patients efficiently and equitably. As the backbone of medicine distribution, pharmaceutical full- line wholesalers provide cost-effective, reliable, and fair access across all regions, ensuring that no patient is left without essential treatments. The Act must be developed in close collaboration with supply chain actors—including pharmaceutical full-line wholesalers, manufacturers, healthcare providers, and regulatory authorities—to ensure that proposed measures are both practical and effective. Rue de la Loi 82 E girp@girp.eu 1040 Brussels, Belgium W www.girp.eu
article_text:
recital 7
However, despite regulatory obligations on marketing authorisation holders to ensure the continuous supply of medicinal products to meet patients’ demand and the additional regulatory mechanism introduced by Regulation of the European Parliament and of the Council (EU) 2022/123 and Regulation (EU) …/… [reference to be added after adoption cf. COM(2023) 193 final] to mitigate and respond to shortages, the functioning of markets alone does not always guarantee the availability of medicinal products. This risk is particularly evident in cases of supply chain disruptions, especially when the supply of a given medicinal product relies on a limited number of global suppliers and production facilities or where there is a high dependency on a single or a limited number of third countries.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: GIRP's feedback specifically addresses the need to strengthen pharmaceutical supply chain resilience and ensure IP/data protection for reporting obligations, and the article acknowledges that market mechanisms alone are insufficient to guarantee medicine availability — reflecting GIRP's position on the need for stronger regulatory intervention in the supply chain.

source_text: Additionally, we believe that regulatory frameworks at the EU level must be designed to allow flexibility under controlled circumstances when shortages arise. Procurement criteria should also reflect the need for a strong and resilient distribution network, leveraging the expertise of pharmaceutical full-line wholesalers to ensure all dispensing points and patients have fair and timely access to medicines. Rue de la Loi 82 E girp@girp.eu 1040 Brussels, Belgium W www.girp.eu

COM(2023) 193 final] to mitigate and respond to shortages, the functioning of markets alone does not always guarantee the availability of medicinal products. source_text: European Healthcare Distribution Association the vital link in healthcare Groupement International de la Répartition Pharmaceutique 3. Ensure the Act complements the measures already proposed by the revision of the EU General Pharmaceutical Legislation (GPL) – Stockpiling focus GIRP find it imperative to reiterate, that in relation to the GPL, it is essential that the pharmaceutical full line wholesaler have the right to be supplied. This would allow pharmaceutical full-line wholesalers to continuously guarantee the appropriate and continued supply of medicines to patients. By ensuring that pharmaceutical full-line wholesalers are supplied, patients will have swift and equitable access to treatments, as these wholesalers can respond to pharmacy orders in an unparalleled timeframe. Hence, part of the goals of the CMA, do need to happen via market failure corrections in the GPL. As mentioned above, GIRP supports the scope of the Critical Medicines Act as defined in the call for evidence namely, to create a more favourable framework for establishing or expanding manufacturing facilities of critical medicines in the EU and to introduce measures that incentivise the use of criteria other than the lowest price when procuring critical medicines. GIRP also supports the essential approach seeing that the CMA will complement the proposed revision of the rules on pharmaceuticals under the EU Pharma Package where the issue of stockpiling or contingency stocks is covered

organisation: European Federation of Pharmaceutical Industries and Associations
source_text: Meeting: Critical Medicines Act
article_text:
article 25
Establishment of Critical Medicines Coordination Group 1.A Critical Medicines Coordination Group (‘Critical Medicines Group’) is hereby established. 2.The Member States and the Commission are Members of the Critical Medicines Group. Each Member State shall appoint a maximum of two high-level permanent representatives, with the expertise relevant for implementing all the different measures set out in this Regulation. Where relevant as regards the function and expertise, Member States may appoint different representatives in relation to different tasks of the Critical Medicines Group. Appointed permanent representatives shall ensure the necessary coordination within their respective Member State. The Agency shall have an observer status. 3.The Critical Medicines Group shall work closely with the MSSG, the Agency, and national authorities responsible for medicinal products. For discussions where input from the medicines regulatory authorities’ perspective is necessary, the Critical Medicines Group may organise joint meetings with the MSSG. 4.The Commission shall organise and coordinate the work of the Critical Medicines Group by means of the Secretariat. 5.A representative of the Commission shall chair the meetings of the Critical Medicines Group. 6.The Critical Medicines Group, at the proposal of the Chair or any its members, may decide to establish a working group. 7.The Critical Medicines Group shall use its best endeavours to reach consensus, where possible. Members with diverging positions may request that their positions and the grounds on which they are based be recorded in the Critical Medicines Group’s position.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The source text only references the 'Critical Medicines Act' as a meeting topic without expressing any specific position on the establishment or structure of a Critical Medicines Coordination Group. The overlap is purely topical.

organisation: European Federation of Pharmaceutical Industries and Associations
source_text: Meeting: Medicines shortages and critical medicines act
article_text:
recital 5
To enhance the security of supply for medicinal products and thereby contribute to a high level of public health protection, the Union has implemented a range of measures that contribute to building a European Health Union. In particular, Regulation (EU) 2022/123 of the European Parliament and of the Council has reinforced the European Medicines Agency’s (‘the Agency’) mandate by enhancing monitoring, coordination, and reporting mechanisms to prevent and mitigate supply disruptions of critical medicinal products across Member States. That Regulation also established the Agency’s Executive Steering Group on Shortages and Safety of Medicinal Products (‘the MSSG’), which brings together representatives from the Agency and Member States, to coordinate urgent actions within the Union to manage existing shortages and issues related to the quality, safety, and efficacy of medicinal products.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The source text is simply a meeting title ('Medicines shortages and critical medicines act') with no expressed position, so the match with Recital 5's substantive content on supply security measures is coincidental and topical only.

organisation: European Federation of Pharmaceutical Industries and Associations
source_text: Meeting: Medicines shortages and critical medicines act
article_text:
recital 37
Ensuring a structured and coordinated approach to strengthening the security of supply of critical medicinal products requires collaboration between the Member States and the Commission. To facilitate this, the Critical Medicines Coordination Group (‘the Critical Medicines Group’) should be established to facilitate effective coordination across the relevant policy areas. The Critical Medicines Group should be composed of high-level representatives of Member States with expertise in medicinal product procurement policies, industrial policy related to pharmaceuticals and public health. The Commission should be a member of the group. To ensure structured discussions, the Commission should chair the Critical Medicines Group and perform the functions of its secretariat.
label: NOISE
reasoning: Again, the source is only a meeting title referencing medicines shortages and the Critical Medicines Act, with no stated position on coordination group governance or composition as described in Recital 37.

organisation: European Federation of Pharmaceutical Industries and Associations
source_text: 3. Clear identification of issues to avoid duplication Given the existing measures and policy proposals the revision of the pharmaceutical legislation will introduce and enforce, the EU’s toolbox for addressing supply security for medicines is already extensive. In 2023, the European Commission set out a package of short-term and medium/longer- term actions to prevent or mitigate critical shortages at the EU level, focusing on the most critical medicines for which supply needs to always be assured in the EU. The CMA could bring great consistency and certainty regarding its evolution, although a regulatory approach could present challenges in terms of flexibility and penalties. It will be key that the objectives of the CMA are clear and do not duplicate those of existing legislations or other policy efforts and initiatives that are already demonstrating results. Many of the tools that could be included within a CMA (the list of critical medicines, a process for assessing vulnerability, risk assessment and reporting requirements, and encouragement of strategic projects) are already underway for medicines. It will be fundamental that the CMA does not create additional layers of complexities to an already complex supply chain ecosystem and that the impact of the CMA is not jeopardized by heterogenous, burdensome and contrasting initiatives/requirements from Member States. 4. Establishing high-level industry benchmarks and targets may send wrong signals
article_text:
recital 5
To enhance the security of supply for medicinal products and thereby contribute to a high level of public health protection, the Union has implemented a range of measures that contribute to building a European Health Union. In particular, Regulation (EU) 2022/123 of the European Parliament and of the Council has reinforced the European Medicines Agency’s (‘the Agency’) mandate by enhancing monitoring, coordination, and reporting mechanisms to prevent and mitigate supply disruptions of critical medicinal products across Member States. That Regulation also established the Agency’s Executive Steering Group on Shortages and Safety of Medicinal Products (‘the MSSG’), which brings together representatives from the Agency and Member States, to coordinate urgent actions within the Union to manage existing shortages and issues related to the quality, safety, and efficacy of medicinal products.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: EFPIA explicitly calls for clarity and consistency in the EU's medicine supply security toolbox, acknowledging existing measures and urging the CMA to build coherently on them — a position clearly reflected in Recital 5, which enumerates the Union's existing supply-security measures and frames the new regulation as part of that ongoing effort.

organisation: European Federation of Pharmaceutical Industries and Associations

Establishing high-level industry benchmarks and targets may send wrong signals
article_text:
recital 16
To ensure that the measures are applied where justified and proportionate, it is necessary to demonstrate that some measures address a vulnerability in the supply chains of a given critical medicinal product. This Regulation should rely on the vulnerability evaluation performed for the purpose of the application of the general pharmaceutical legislation as per Regulation (EU) No …/… [reference to be added after adoption cf. COM(2023) 193 final]. To detect a vulnerability in the supply chains it is necessary to look at aggregated data across all medicinal products authorised in the Union and containing the same active substance, route of administration and formulation. Such an approach allows for the determination whether, for a critical medicinal product with a given active substance, the Union is highly dependent on a single or a limited number of third countries, or a limited number of sites, for active substances, key inputs, or finished dosage forms.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: EFPIA's call for clear identification of issues and avoidance of duplication across existing measures aligns with Recital 16's emphasis on ensuring measures are justified and proportionate and that the new regulation relies on vulnerability evaluations already established under existing pharmaceutical legislation, avoiding redundancy.

organisation: European Federation of Pharmaceutical Industries and Associations
source_text: 2. Incentivise innovative technologies and supporting infrastructure that can help to address supply chain vulnerabilities. These technologies include continuous manufacturing (for both active ingredients and medicinal products), portable, modular manufacturing facilities, and single-use-systems that enable faster and agile manufacturing. 3. Streamline regulatory processes and extensive supply requirements, including by applying modern digital technologies to regulatory processes and minimising variations for manufacturing change authorisations (i.e. type 2 variations). 4. End national stockpiling requirements at finished product level and, as alternatives: a. To ensure that safety stockpiles are effective and proportional to their purpose, establish maximum thresholds for the entire EU region, including specific thresholds by product categories according to the identified vulnerabilities of critical medicines’ supply chains; and b. Leverage EU solidarity mechanisms between Member States that would allow marketing authorisation holders to quickly redeploy inventories, therefore avoiding uneven distribution of stocks. 5. Include a guidance on medicines public procurement that integrates inter alia security of supply, multi-winner tenders, MEAT (Most Economically Advantageous Tender) criteria, and codifying this guidance in the Public Procurement Directive when it is revised. 6
article_text:
recital 10
The smooth functioning of the internal market and a high level of protection of human health should be ensured as regards medicinal products and it should be aimed to complementing other Union pharmaceutical legislation by providing for a harmonised framework supporting Member States’ coordinated efforts to encourage investments in new and existing manufacturing capacities for critical medicinal products, encouraging the strategic use of public procurement instruments by the Member States as well as the coordination of the Member States’ approaches, including through leveraging aggregated demand through Commission facilitated collaborative procurement procedures of critical medicinal products and medicinal products of common interest. Due to the international dimension of the security of supply, in particular taking into account that diversification of supply chains and an overall increase of supply are elements of a solution for ensuring the security of supply, international cooperation should be encouraged.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: EFPIA explicitly advocates for incentivising innovative manufacturing technologies, streamlining regulatory processes, and coordinating Member State procurement approaches — positions directly reflected in Recital 10's language about harmonised frameworks, encouraging investment in manufacturing capacities, and leveraging coordinated public procurement instruments.

source_text: Meeting: Critical Medicines Act, Urban Water Waste Directive
article_text:
recital 19
The production of medicinal products has environmental implications and may negatively impact not only the environment itself but also human health. The environmental assessments and authorisations required under Union law are an integral part of the permit-granting process for strategic projects and an essential safeguard to ensure negative environmental impacts are prevented or minimised. However, to ensure that permit-granting processes for strategic projects are predictable and timely, it should be possible to streamline the required assessments and authorisations by the relevant authority, while not lowering the level of environmental protection.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The pre-proposal meeting covered both the Critical Medicines Act and the Urban Waste Water Directive; Recital 19 directly addresses the environmental impact of medicinal product production and permit-granting streamlining, which sits at the intersection of both topics Sandoz raised, suggesting genuine position alignment rather than coincidental overlap.

source_text: Meeting: Generic Medicines, Critical Medicines Act
article_text:
recital 15
A well-defined list of critical medicinal products is essential to ensure that the measures are targeted, effective, and proportionate. The critical medicinal products covered by this Regulation should be those for which insufficient supply results in serious harm or risk of serious harm to patients. For this reason this Regulation should apply to critical medicinal products on the Union list of critical medicinal products, as established by Regulation (EU) …/… [reference to be added after adoption cf. COM(2023) 193 final]. That list builds upon the experiences of the European Medicines Agency and Member States’ Agencies that in 2024, in anticipation of the reform of pharmaceutical legislation, identified a list of 276 critical medicinal products.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Teva's meeting explicitly concerned the Critical Medicines Act and generic medicines, and Recital 15 directly addresses the establishment of a well-defined list of critical medicinal products under the Critical Medicines Act framework. The organisation's engagement with this topic aligns with the article's substance.

article_text:
recital 5
To enhance the security of supply for medicinal products and thereby contribute to a high level of public health protection, the Union has implemented a range of measures that contribute to building a European Health Union. In particular, Regulation (EU) 2022/123 of the European Parliament and of the Council has reinforced the European Medicines Agency’s (‘the Agency’) mandate by enhancing monitoring, coordination, and reporting mechanisms to prevent and mitigate supply disruptions of critical medicinal products across Member States. That Regulation also established the Agency’s Executive Steering Group on Shortages and Safety of Medicinal Products (‘the MSSG’), which brings together representatives from the Agency and Member States, to coordinate urgent actions within the Union to manage existing shortages and issues related to the quality, safety, and efficacy of medicinal products.

source_text: 2 Well-defined and realistic sustainable procurement schemes that are designed in collaboration with the industry • Introduction of multi-criteria and multi-winner tenders, which will resilience of the supply chain and the overall contribution to the European economy Safeguarding Develop dynamic pricing practices critical Safeguarding critical medicines from the unintended consequences of environmental legislation sustainability • Any legislation with a potential impact on the economic viability of critical medicines must be accompanied by a thorough impact assessment to prevent any future disruptions such as the ones stemming from the Urban Waster Water Treatment Directive. 28 |
article_text:
article 18
Incentivising resilience, sustainability and positive social impacts in public procurement procedures 1.For award procedures of critical medicinal products falling within the scope of Directive 2014/24/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council, contracting authorities in the Member States shall apply procurement requirements other than price-only award criteria such as procurement requirements that promote the resilience of supply in the Union. Those procurement requirements shall be defined in accordance with Directive 2014/24/EU and may relate to stockholding obligations, the number of diversified suppliers, monitoring of supply chains, their transparency to the contracting authority and contract performance clauses on timely delivery. 2.With regard to critical medicinal products for which a vulnerability in the supply chains has been confirmed through a vulnerability evaluation pointing to the high level of dependency on a single or a limited number of third countries, the contracting authorities shall, where justified, apply procurement requirements that favour suppliers that manufacture a significant proportion of these critical medicinal products in the Union. These requirements shall be applied in compliance with the Union’s international commitments. 3.With regard to other medicinal products of common interest, where justified by market analysis and public health considerations, the contracting authorities may apply procurement requirements that favour suppliers that manufacture at least a significant proportion of these medicinal products in the Union. These requirements shall be applied in compliance with the Union’s international commitments. 4.This Article shall not preclude contracting authorities from using additional qualitative requirements, including in relation to environmental sustainability and social rights. 5.Contracting authorities may exceptionally decide not to apply paragraphs 1, 2 and 3 where justified by market analysis or considerations related to the financing of health services.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Teva explicitly advocated for multi-criteria and multi-winner tenders and sustainable procurement schemes designed with industry input, which is directly reflected in Article 18's requirement for contracting authorities to apply procurement criteria beyond price-only, promoting supply chain resilience.

article_text:
recital 7
However, despite regulatory obligations on marketing authorisation holders to ensure the continuous supply of medicinal products to meet patients’ demand and the additional regulatory mechanism introduced by Regulation of the European Parliament and of the Council (EU) 2022/123 and Regulation (EU) …/… [reference to be added after adoption cf. COM(2023) 193 final] to mitigate and respond to shortages, the functioning of markets alone does not always guarantee the availability of medicinal products. This risk is particularly evident in cases of supply chain disruptions, especially when the supply of a given medicinal product relies on a limited number of global suppliers and production facilities or where there is a high dependency on a single or a limited number of third countries.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Teva's feedback on reducing dependencies and enhancing resilience of critical medicines supply aligns with Recital 7's acknowledgment that market functioning alone does not guarantee medicine availability and that supply chain disruptions pose a particular risk, supporting the need for the regulatory measures Teva was advocating.
